title: Newton's First Law
subtitle: Objects at rest stay at rest

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A ball is rolling across a smooth, flat surface with no friction. What happens to its speed?
options: It gradually slows down and stops
It keeps moving at the same speed
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception — students often think objects need a force to keep moving. Newton’s First Law says an object in motion stays in motion at constant speed UNLESS acted on by an unbalanced force. No friction means no unbalanced force, so the ball rolls on forever.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! With no friction (no unbalanced force), the ball continues at the same speed forever. This is Newton’s First Law — also called the law of inertia.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: Forces misconception #1 — Institute of Physics Teaching Resources

steps.title: Newton’s First Law
steps.bodyHTML: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at constant speed in a straight line, UNLESS acted on by a resultant force. This is inertia — the tendency to resist changes in motion. Heavier objects have more inertia. A change in speed or direction both require a resultant force.

variables.label: Moving object continues at constant velocity
Resultant force needed to change motion

relationships: No resultant force → no change in motion
Resultant force → acceleration (speed or direction changes)

commonErrors: Thinking a force is needed to keep an object moving
Confusing constant speed with zero speed
Forgetting that direction change also needs a force

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Set the force to zero and watch what happens — the object keeps moving at constant velocity. Then increase the force to see how acceleration changes motion. This helps you understand N1L: when F = 0, velocity stays the same.

aqa.questionText: A car is travelling at a constant speed of 30 m/s on a straight road. Explain, using Newton’s First Law, what this tells us about the forces acting on the car.

steps.label: State Newton’s First Law
steps.content: An object remains at constant velocity (or at rest) unless acted on by a resultant force.

steps.label: Apply to the car
steps.content: The car is travelling at constant speed in a straight line — its velocity is not changing.

steps.label: Conclude about forces
steps.content: Therefore the resultant force on the car must be zero. The driving force equals the total resistive forces (friction + air resistance).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — concludes resultant force = 0 / forces are balanced

aqa.finalAnswer: The resultant force on the car is zero because it travels at constant velocity.

edexcel.questionText: A spacecraft is moving through deep space where there is no friction or air resistance. Explain why it continues to move at constant velocity.

steps.label: Identify the forces
steps.content: In deep space there is no friction or air resistance, so no forces act on the spacecraft. The resultant force is zero.

steps.label: Apply N1L
steps.content: By Newton’s First Law, with no resultant force acting, the spacecraft continues at constant velocity (same speed, same direction).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — links zero resultant force to constant velocity via N1L

edexcel.finalAnswer: No resultant force acts, so by N1L the spacecraft continues at constant velocity.

ocr-gateway.source: OCR Gateway Style · Forces · N1L Terminal Velocity
ocr-gateway.questionText: A skydiver reaches terminal velocity. Use Newton’s First Law to explain why her speed stays constant.

steps.label: Describe the forces
steps.content: At terminal velocity, the weight (downward) equals the air resistance (upward).

steps.label: State the resultant force
steps.content: Since the forces are balanced, the resultant force is zero.

steps.label: Apply N1L
steps.content: By Newton’s First Law, with zero resultant force, the skydiver continues at constant speed (terminal velocity).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — links to N1L and constant speed

ocr-gateway.finalAnswer: Weight equals air resistance, so resultant force is zero, and by N1L speed stays constant.

ocr-21c.source: OCR 21st Century Style · Forces · N1L Friction
ocr-21c.questionText: A hockey puck slides across ice at constant speed. Explain why it eventually stops, using Newton’s First Law.

steps.label: Identify the unbalanced force
steps.content: Although ice has very low friction, there is still a small friction force acting backward on the puck. This is an unbalanced (resultant) force.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — identifies friction as an unbalanced force

steps.label: Apply N1L
steps.content: By Newton’s First Law, an object only changes its motion when acted on by a resultant force. The friction force decelerates the puck until it stops.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — uses N1L to explain deceleration

ocr-21c.finalAnswer: Friction provides a resultant force that, by N1L, changes the puck’s motion until it stops.

wjec.questionText: A book sits on a table. Use Newton’s First Law to explain why it does not move.

steps.label: Identify the forces
steps.content: The weight of the book acts downward. The normal reaction force from the table acts upward. These two forces are equal and opposite, so the resultant force is zero.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — identifies balanced forces / resultant force = 0

steps.label: Apply N1L
steps.content: By Newton’s First Law, an object at rest stays at rest if the resultant force is zero. So the book remains stationary.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — links zero resultant force to remaining at rest via N1L

wjec.finalAnswer: Weight and normal reaction are balanced (resultant force = 0), so by N1L the book stays at rest.

aqa.questionText: What does Newton’s First Law predict about an object with zero resultant force?

options.label: A
options.text: It must be stationary

options.label: B
options.text: It will decelerate and stop

options.label: C
options.text: It stays at rest or continues at constant velocity

options.label: D
options.text: It accelerates in the direction of its motion

markSchemeSteps: N1L: zero resultant force means no change in motion
Object stays at rest OR continues at constant velocity
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark for stating N1L · 1 mark for correct answer (C)
aqa.commonMistake: Option A is the most common wrong answer — students forget that zero resultant force also applies to objects already moving at constant velocity.

edexcel.questionText: A train moves at constant velocity. What can you say about the driving force and friction?

options.label: A
options.text: The driving force is greater than friction

options.label: B
options.text: The driving force equals friction

options.label: C
options.text: Friction is greater than the driving force

options.label: D
options.text: There is no friction acting

markSchemeSteps: Constant velocity means zero resultant force (N1L)
edexcel.markSchemeAward: 1 mark for zero resultant force · 1 mark for driving force = friction
edexcel.commonMistake: Option A is tempting — students think you need a bigger driving force to keep moving. N1L says constant velocity means balanced forces.

ocr-gateway.source: OCR Gateway Style · Forces · N1L Practice
ocr-gateway.questionText: Which of these is an example of Newton’s First Law?

options.label: A
options.text: A rocket accelerates as its engines fire

options.label: B
options.text: A book remains still on a desk because the forces on it are balanced

options.label: C
options.text: A ball falls faster and faster due to gravity

options.label: D
options.text: A car slows down when the brakes are applied

markSchemeSteps: N1L applies when resultant force is zero
Book on desk: weight = normal reaction, so resultant force = 0 and it stays at rest
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark for identifying balanced forces · 1 mark for correct answer (B)
ocr-gateway.commonMistake: Options A, C, and D all involve a resultant force causing acceleration or deceleration — these are Newton’s Second Law, not First.

ocr-21c.source: OCR 21st Century Style · Forces · N1L Practice
ocr-21c.questionText: A satellite orbits Earth at constant speed. Is there a resultant force acting on it?

options.label: A
options.text: No — constant speed means zero resultant force

options.label: B
options.text: Yes — gravity acts as a centripetal force, changing its direction

options.label: C
options.text: No — it is in a vacuum so no forces act

options.label: D
options.text: Yes — the satellite is accelerating forwards

markSchemeSteps: Constant speed does NOT mean constant velocity — direction is changing
A changing direction means a resultant force acts (gravity provides centripetal force)
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark for identifying direction change · 1 mark for correct answer (B)
ocr-21c.commonMistake: Option A is the classic trap — students confuse constant speed with constant velocity. The satellite’s direction changes, so a resultant force must act.

wjec.questionText: A box on a smooth floor is pushed and then released. What happens to the box after it is released?

options.label: A
options.text: It immediately stops

options.label: B
options.text: It gradually slows down due to its weight

options.label: C
options.text: It continues at constant velocity

options.label: D
options.text: It speeds up because it was given energy

markSchemeSteps: Smooth floor = no friction, so no resultant force after release
By N1L, the box continues at constant velocity
wjec.markSchemeAward: 1 mark for no resultant force · 1 mark for correct answer (C)
wjec.commonMistake: Option A is the most common error — students think objects stop when you stop pushing. N1L says no force means no change in motion.

steps.title: Newton’s First Law — no equation, but key idea

equation.examTip: N1L questions are usually ‘explain’ questions worth 2–3 marks. Always mention: resultant force is zero → acceleration is zero → constant speed (or stays at rest).

boardNotes.aqa: N1L is not a formula — it’s a law stated in words. AQA expects you to explain using the phrase ‘resultant force’.
boardNotes.edexcel: Edexcel often tests N1L through terminal velocity and constant speed scenarios. Always link to ‘balanced forces’.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: OCR Gateway may ask you to describe examples of N1L in everyday life. Mention inertia by name.
boardNotes.ocr-21c: OCR 21st Century links N1L to the concept of inertia and may ask about inertial mass.
boardNotes.wjec: WJEC expects clear use of the term ‘resultant force’ and application to stationary and moving objects.
